C1.evidence: Turn 3: 'Okay, give me just a moment...' — pure filler with no follow-through. Turn 7: 'I see. You're saying there's an issue with the services' — trailing fragment, no question mark, no complete thought. Turn 13: 'Okay.' — single-word filler conveying zero information after a substantive customer statement.
C1.reasoning: Multiple turns (3, 7, 13) are either pure filler or incomplete fragments. While most other turns are coherent, these zero-information turns noticeably disrupted the flow when the borrower was sharing important dispute information.

C2.evidence: Customer speaks English throughout (Turns 2, 5, 6, 8, 9, 11, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20). Agent responds in English throughout (Turns 1, 3, 4, 7, 10, 13, 15, 17, 19, 21). No language switch requested.
C2.reasoning: Both parties speak English for the entire call; no language mismatch or inappropriate mixing occurred.

C3.evidence: Turn 13: Customer mid-sentence ('And I told clearly we paid the already amount and') — agent responds 'Okay.' which is non-responsive. Turn 15: After customer completes the claim that the rest 'should be canceled,' agent pivots to credit score impact rather than engaging with the cancellation claim. Turn 19: Agent deflects to external contact rather than addressing customer's legitimate dispute.
C3.reasoning: Turn 13 is a clear failure to respond to substantive customer input, and Turn 15 avoids directly engaging with the core dispute claim (cancellation of balance). Most other turns do address what the customer said, preventing a 'not_met' verdict.

C4.evidence: Turn 1: Agent identifies as 'Alex from DemoCompany.' Turn 4: Agent uses customer name 'Arjun Nair.' No company or name confusion throughout.
C4.reasoning: Correct borrower name and agent identity used consistently throughout the call with no confusion.

P1.evidence: Turn 1: Greeting mentions 'charges on your account' but no specific amounts. Turn 2: Customer responds ('Hire'). Turn 4: Agent proceeds to discovery without disclosing amounts. Amounts first appear in Turn 17 ('fifty thousand rupees'). No explicit identity confirmation ('Am I speaking with Arjun Nair?') before proceeding.
P1.reasoning: No amounts were disclosed before the borrower spoke, satisfying P1(a). However, identity was never explicitly confirmed — the agent inferred identity and moved forward after 'Hire,' which is a meaningful gap in P1(b), especially for a high-DPD account.

P2.evidence: Turn 17: 'remove some of the penalty charges from your fifty thousand rupees' — fifty thousand matches TOS. POS is listed as zero, which was never quoted. No literal 'POS' or 'TOS' spoken. Turn 19: 'Since this is beyond what I can resolve, I recommend reaching out to DemoLender directly' — closely mirrors the forbidden pattern 'For anything else, contact the relevant team.'
P2.reasoning: No POS/TOS terminology slips and the amount quoted matches TOS. However, Turn 19 uses language functionally identical to the forbidden phrase, which is a clear protocol violation.

P3.evidence: Turn 15: 'an unpaid loan can still negatively affect your credit score. Banks see it as an outstanding amount.' — authorized by system prompt. No threats of legal action, property seizure, or wage garnishment anywhere in the transcript.
P3.reasoning: All consequence language used (credit score impact) is within the bounds authorized by the system prompt. No coercive or threatening language was employed.

P4.evidence: Turn 13: After customer states 'And I told clearly we paid the already amount and' — agent says 'Okay.' This is a filler response but not an inappropriate 'Hello?'. No instances of 'Hello?' after complete customer statements. No silence handling needed.
P4.reasoning: No inappropriate 'Hello?' or 'Are you there?' was used after a complete customer statement. The silence protocol was not triggered in this call.

S1.evidence: Turn 5-6: 'disconnected with the institution… not happy with services.' Turn 9: 'Admissions were canceled, took admission somewhere else.' Turns 12-14: 'paid the already amount… rest of the balance amount to be canceled.' Function call proceed_to_dispute after Turn 8. Call disposition is marked DISPUTE.
S1.reasoning: The agent correctly identified this as an institute dispute — the function call proceed_to_dispute was triggered appropriately at Turn 8. The situation classification was accurate.

S2.evidence: Turn 15: After dispute signals, agent pushes credit score impact — 'even though there's a dispute, an unpaid loan can still negatively affect your credit score.' Turn 17: Agent explicitly tries to get payment — 'settling the payment doesn't mean you're giving up on your dispute… you can still pursue that separately while also stopping any further credit damage.' Turn 19: Finally redirects to DemoLender support.
S2.reasoning: The system prompt for disputes says 'route to dispute handling, do NOT push payment.' The agent called proceed_to_dispute correctly but then immediately pivoted to payment pressure (Turns 15, 17) rather than staying in dispute-handling mode. The eventual redirect to support@demolender.com was appropriate but came after inappropriate payment pressure.

S3.evidence: Turn 8: Dispute signal from 'admission captured' — agent correctly calls proceed_to_dispute. Turn 9: Clearer dispute signal ('admissions canceled, took admission elsewhere') — agent calls proceed_to_negotiation immediately, abandoning dispute handling. Turn 15: No pivot toward dispute resolution; instead applies credit pressure.
S3.reasoning: The initial pivot to dispute was correct (Turn 8), but the agent abandoned it within one turn (calling proceed_to_negotiation after Turn 9) and then applied payment pressure — the opposite of appropriate adaptation for a dispute caller.

S4.evidence: Turn 18: Customer clearly states 'Why do I have to pay? They disconnected with the institution' — firm refusal/dispute restatement. end_call was already fired after Turn 17 (before Turn 18). Turn 20: Customer is mid-sentence ('I will try to...') when the call ends at Turn 21.
S4.reasoning: end_call was prematurely fired after Turn 17 while the customer still had more to say (Turn 18, Turn 20). However, the agent did exit relatively promptly and used the correct 'dispute_unresolved' reason. The premature firing is a meaningful gap but not catastrophic.

F1.evidence: Phases visited: opening → discovery → dispute → negotiation. proceed_to_discovery after Turn 3 (before amounts disclosed). proceed_to_dispute after Turn 8. proceed_to_negotiation after Turn 9 — immediately after entering dispute phase. No proper closing phase.
F1.reasoning: The dispute phase was visited but almost immediately abandoned (proceed_to_negotiation after just one exchange in dispute mode). Moving from dispute to negotiation without resolving the dispute or even exploring it fully is an inappropriate phase progression.

F2.evidence: Turn 4: Agent asks 'what's been causing the delay?' — borrower responds. Turns 5-9: Borrower provides real information about institution disconnection and canceled admission. Turns 12-14: Borrower explains partial payment and cancellation claim. proceed_to_negotiation called after Turn 9.
F2.reasoning: At least 2-3 substantive exchanges occurred where the borrower gave real information about their situation before the transition to negotiation. Discovery was brief but not a mere formality.

F3.evidence: Turn 5-6: 'We have disconnected with the institution. We are not happy with the services.' Turn 9: 'Admissions were canceled, and we took an admission in some other institution.' These constitute explicit institute dispute signals. proceed_to_dispute called after Turn 8.
F3.reasoning: The borrower explicitly stated the institution disconnected and admissions were canceled — these are legitimate dispute triggers matching the system prompt criteria. The proceed_to_dispute was correctly called, not triggered on mere clarification questions.

F4.evidence: end_call fired after Turn 17. Turns 18, 19, 20, 21 all continued: Turn 18 — customer makes firm statement; Turn 19 — agent responds substantively; Turn 20 — customer still mid-sentence; Turn 21 — agent closes. That is 3 substantive turns after end_call.
F4.reasoning: end_call was triggered prematurely (after Turn 17) while the borrower still had active, substantive things to say (Turns 18 and 20). Four more turns continued after the function call, well exceeding the 2-turn grace period for goodbyes.

Q1.evidence: Turn 17 is the only turn where an amount ('fifty thousand rupees') is mentioned. No repeated pitches or cycling through the same script segments beyond one credit score mention (Turn 15) and one payment suggestion (Turn 17).
Q1.reasoning: Amounts were stated only once and no scripted segment was repeated more than once. The call was too short for looping to become an issue.

Q2.evidence: Turn 21: 'I've noted that' — implies the agent has recorded information, which is a minor unverifiable claim. No payment plans, installment options, or unauthorized arrangements mentioned. support@demolender.com is authorized in the system prompt.
Q2.reasoning: 'I've noted that' is a minor and harmless conversational phrase. No substantive fabrications about payment facilities, capabilities, or outcomes were made.

Q3.evidence: No instance in the transcript where the agent re-asks for information the borrower already provided. The agent asked once about payment delay (Turn 4) and did not revisit that question.
Q3.reasoning: No redundant questions were asked; the agent moved forward based on information already provided.

Q4.evidence: Turn 13: Customer says 'And I told clearly we paid the already amount and' — agent responds 'Okay.' with no genuine engagement. Turn 15: After customer says rest should be 'canceled' — agent immediately pivots to credit score talk without acknowledging the cancellation claim. Turn 10: Acknowledgment references specific content but ends with generic 'dissatisfied with how things turned out.'
Q4.reasoning: Turn 13's 'Okay' is a genuine failure to acknowledge substantive customer input, and Turn 15 completely ignores the cancellation claim to pivot to credit pressure. These are meaningful gaps, though Turn 10 and Turn 17 show partial genuine acknowledgment.

Q5.evidence: Turn 17: 'I understand your point of view. Since this is beyond what I can resolve... settling the payment doesn't mean you're giving up on your dispute with the institution. You can still pursue that separately while also stopping any further credit damage. We might even be able to remove some of the penalty charges from your fifty thousand rupees.' — approximately 65 words, multiple messages bundled.
Q5.reasoning: Turn 17 exceeds 50 words and bundles multiple ideas (empathy, dispute separation, payment justification, charge removal) in one voice utterance, which is difficult to absorb in a phone call. Other turns are appropriately concise.

E1.evidence: Turn 10: 'I understand you're not happy with the services from the institution and have disconnected with them' — references specific content. Turn 15: 'I hear you' but then immediately applies credit pressure to a dispute customer. Turn 17: 'I understand your point of view' then pushes payment.
E1.reasoning: Turn 10 provides a moderately specific acknowledgment of the borrower's situation, but Turns 15 and 17 follow acknowledgments with immediate pressure pivots, showing the empathy is formulaic rather than substantive.

E2.evidence: DPD is 180 (NPA territory). Customer is actively disputing — not stalling with ability to pay. Turn 15: Credit score warning applied to dispute customer. Turn 17: Payment push despite clear dispute signals ('Why do I have to pay?' at Turn 18 confirms customer felt pressured).
E2.reasoning: Applying credit score pressure (Turn 15) and a payment pitch (Turn 17) to a customer in active dispute with a legitimate institute grievance is inappropriate pressure calibration. The customer's reaction in Turn 18 confirms this crossed a line.

E3.evidence: Turn 13: Customer is mid-sentence ('And I told clearly we paid the already amount and') — agent cuts in with 'Okay.' Turn 20: Customer is mid-sentence ('You can please share a message. I will try to') — call ends at Turn 21 without letting customer finish. end_call had already been fired after Turn 17.
E3.reasoning: Two instances where the agent cut off the customer mid-sentence — Turn 13 with a non-response and Turn 20 where the call ends while the customer is still speaking. These are meaningful interruptions of conversational space.

E4.evidence: Customer expresses frustration (Turn 18: 'Why do I have to pay?'). Agent responds professionally in Turn 19 by redirecting to support contact. No aggression, sarcasm, or condescension throughout.
E4.reasoning: The agent maintained a professional and respectful tone throughout, even when the customer expressed frustration, and did not escalate or match any hostility.

O1.evidence: Turn 19: Agent provides support@demolender.com. Turn 20: Customer says 'You can please share a message. I will try to' — implying willingness to contact but no specific action confirmed. Turn 21: Call ends with 'Take care.' No specific next step (date, format, what to say to DemoLender) confirmed by borrower.
O1.reasoning: The call ends with a vague direction to contact support@demolender.com, but no concrete next step was confirmed by the borrower (what to write, expected timeline, case reference). The customer was mid-sentence when the call ended, suggesting the resolution was incomplete.

O2.evidence: proceed_to_discovery called after Turn 3 (before amounts disclosed — premature). proceed_to_dispute after Turn 8 — appropriate. proceed_to_negotiation after Turn 9 — very premature (dispute phase barely started). end_call with 'dispute_unresolved' after Turn 17 — reason matches situation but timing was premature (customer still actively engaged).
O2.reasoning: The end_call reason ('dispute_unresolved') correctly describes the situation, but it was triggered prematurely mid-conversation. proceed_to_negotiation was called far too early (after one dispute exchange), and proceed_to_discovery was also premature.

O3.evidence: Customer had a legitimate dispute and was cooperative in explaining it (Turns 5-9, 12-14, 16). Agent applied payment pressure (Turns 15, 17) on a dispute caller, prompting increased frustration ('Why do I have to pay?' — Turn 18). Customer got support@demolender.com but their mid-sentence close (Turn 20) suggests incomplete resolution.
O3.reasoning: The agent's payment pressure on a dispute caller likely increased frustration rather than helping. While the customer was given an actionable email, the mishandled dispute handling and premature call exit left the borrower without clear guidance on their dispute path.

worst_messages.agent_message: Right. And I understand you've already taken steps to address this. Just so you know, settling the payment doesn't mean you're giving up on your dispute with the institution. You can still pursue that separately while also stopping any further credit damage. We might even be able to remove some of the penalty charges from your fifty thousand rupees.
worst_messages.issue: The agent bundles a superficial empathy line with a direct payment pitch aimed at a borrower in active, legitimate dispute. This turn argues the customer should pay despite a disputed cancellation — which is the opposite of dispute-handling protocol. It also quotes the full TOS as a 'removal' carrot rather than exploring the dispute. The end_call function was fired immediately after this turn while the customer still had substantive things to say (Turns 18 and 20), making this the pivot point that ended the call prematurely.
worst_messages.should_have_done: Acknowledge the cancellation claim directly and explain what the borrower can do through the lender's dispute channel. Something like: 'That's important — if the institution cancelled the admission, DemoLender needs to know that formally. Do you have documentation of the cancellation or the notice you mentioned? I can flag this on my end and you can also email support@demolender.com with those documents so they can review your case.'
worst_messages.impact: The payment pressure immediately antagonised the borrower (Turn 18: 'Why why do I have to pay?'), confirming the approach backfired. The premature end_call meant the agent then had to awkwardly continue for four more turns after the function was already fired, and the call ended with the customer still mid-sentence, leaving the dispute completely unresolved and the borrower without a concrete action path.

worst_messages.agent_message: I hear you. I want to mention that even though there's a dispute, an unpaid loan can still negatively affect your credit score. Banks see it as an outstanding amount.
worst_messages.issue: This is the first pressure tactic deployed on a caller who has just clearly articulated a legitimate institute-level dispute (cancelled admission, prior partial payment, notice served). The system prompt for disputes explicitly says not to push payment. Using credit score consequences as leverage against a disputed loan is both a protocol violation and an inappropriate pressure calibration — it treats a grievance as a financial-behaviour problem.
worst_messages.should_have_done: Stay in dispute-handling mode and probe the dispute details rather than pivoting to consequences. For example: 'I understand — and that notice is important. Can you tell me more about when the admission was cancelled and whether DemoLender was informed at the time? That context will help determine the right path forward.'
worst_messages.impact: This set up the adversarial dynamic that produced the frustrated 'Why why do I have to pay?' in Turn 18. It also signalled to the borrower that the agent was not genuinely engaging with the dispute, undermining any remaining trust and making the eventual redirect to support@demolender.com feel dismissive rather than helpful.

worst_messages.issue: The borrower in Turn 12 disclosed a critical piece of dispute information — that they had already made a partial payment and expected the remaining balance to be cancelled. A bare 'Okay' completely ignores this substantive input. This was the most actionable detail in the entire call and deserved direct follow-up: confirmation of the partial payment amount, when it was made, and what cancellation was promised or agreed.
worst_messages.should_have_done: Engage directly with the partial payment claim: 'You mentioned you already paid part of it — do you know roughly how much and when that was? And was there a written agreement or communication from DemoLender or the institution about cancelling the rest?'
worst_messages.impact: Because this information was never explored, the agent had no facts to work with when the dispute escalated. The partial payment claim and the cancellation expectation were the core of the borrower's position, and ignoring them forced the borrower to repeat and escalate in Turn 14, and ultimately contributed to the agent skipping past dispute resolution entirely into an inappropriate payment pitch.
